summary: Fowler argues that SOA, like all software design, should embrace evolutionary rather than planned design approaches. He challenges the assumption that enterprise service interfaces must be designed upfront because they're hard to change, noting that unpredictable change is inevitable even at enterprise scale. The key question is whether change is predictable—if not, planned design leads to the same chaos it seeks to prevent, often with significant sunk costs. He advocates for approaches like Consumer Driven Contracts and Guerilla SOA that build systems incrementally based on business value. The web itself serves as proof that evolutionary design can work at massive scale despite being messy.
key_points: Planned design only works when change is predictable; in enterprise contexts, unpredictability is doubled
Sunk costs in planned SOA designs often undermine the business value they were meant to produce
Consumer Driven Contracts enable evolutionary thinking about service interfaces
Guerilla SOA builds services through small, value-driven steps rather than big upfront architecture
The web proves evolutionary design can scale far beyond typical SOA efforts
money_quotes: The fundamental question I ask is 'is change predictable?' Only if change is predictable is a planned design approach valid.
If predictability is hard within the context of a single application, it's doubly hard across an enterprise.
If we use planned design in an unpredictable context we find that however good the plans are, they will be undermined by the unpredictable changes, leading to the same mess we are currently in.
The whole point of loose coupling is to make change easier.
The web is built on very loose coupling and lots of unpredictable changes. It is, in many ways, a mess—but it's a mess that works, delivering real value to lots of people every day.
key_insight: Since enterprise change is inherently unpredictable, SOA must embrace evolutionary design—the web itself proves this messy approach delivers real value at scale.
